script: I never intended to spend the night in the old mill at the edge of town, but a dare from a friend and my own curiosity got the better of me one late October evening. The mill had been abandoned for over fifty years, its windows shattered, the roof partially caved in, and vines curling up the sides like dark fingers. Everyone in town had stories about it: strange lights at night, shadows moving behind broken windows, and whispers that carried through the empty rooms if you dared to get close. I arrived just before sunset, the sky a bruised mix of purple and orange, and the wind cold enough to make me pull my jacket tighter around me. Pushing open the splintered door, I stepped inside and immediately felt the heavy, damp smell of rot and mold. My flashlight cut through the darkness, illuminating wooden beams cracked with age, piles of dust-covered sacks, and machinery rusted into uselessness. As I explored, I heard the faintest creak behind me and froze, heart hammering, convinced it was a branch moving in the wind. But the sound repeated, coming from deeper inside the mill, and it wasn’t random. Step by step, I moved forward, following the noise to the top floor where the old conveyor belts and grinding wheels stood frozen in time. There, in the corner, I saw it: a shadow that didn’t belong, tall and thin, its outline flickering as though it couldn’t hold a solid form. I thought I might be hallucinating, but when it turned toward me, I realized the space where its eyes should have been was an endless void, blacker than the shadows around it. I tried to speak, but my voice caught in my throat, and when I took a step back, the figure moved closer, silently, gliding over the old wooden floorboards that groaned under no visible weight. Panic set in, and I turned to flee, but the stairwell behind me seemed longer than it should, the ceiling lower, the walls somehow narrower. My flashlight flickered, then died, leaving me in almost complete darkness, except for the faint, pulsating glow of the shadow, which now seemed to radiate a cold so intense it made my skin ache. I stumbled forward blindly, my hands scraping against splintered beams and rusty equipment, and every step I took was met with another creak behind me, as if invisible feet were following, matching my pace perfectly. I reached the mill’s main exit, only to find the door had disappeared, replaced by a solid wall of rotting wood that I hadn’t noticed before. I screamed, pounding my fists, trying to push through, but the wall was unyielding. When I turned around, the shadow was closer, now joined by others—dozens of tall, thin shapes, all moving toward me silently, their empty voids where eyes should have been trained on me. I backed into a corner, trembling, convinced I would die there, when I remembered the loose hatch in the floor that led down to the basement. I dove through it just as the shadows reached the top floor, landing hard on cold, damp stones, my flashlight broken but still clutched in my hand. In the basement, it was even darker, the smell stronger, and the sound of water dripping somewhere echoing unnaturally. I crawled along the floor, following a faint draft that promised an exit, and finally found a small side door. I threw it open and tumbled out into the night, gasping, covered in dirt and scratches, and when I looked back, the mill looked empty, still, and harmless in the moonlight.
